key_sections.introduction: This case is on remand following the appeal of our prior final written decision (Paper 78, “Decision”). In the Decision, we found that none of the challenged claims 1–6 of U.S. Patent No. 7,041,786 B2 (Ex. 1001, “the ’786 patent”) had been shown unpatentable because while Petitioner $^{2}$ had established a prima facie showing of obvivousness, that showing was “outweighed by Patent Owner’s comparatively strong evidence demonstrating unexpected results.” Dec. 60. Those findings and our conclusion on obviousness were appealed to the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit (“Federal Circuit”). See Paper 83 (“CAFC Decision”). The Federal Circuit did not disturb our determination that Petitioner “established a prima facie case of obviousness.” See CAFC Dec. 6–8. However, it took issue with the unexpected results analysis in the Decision, explaining that “without more explanation from the Board as to its determination that the experiments [offered by Patent Owner to show unexpected results] compared the challenged claims to the closest prior art, we cannot reach a judgment on MSN’s challenges to the Board’s holding of non-obviousness.” Id. at 14. Accordingly, the Federal Circuit vacated the Decision and remanded the case for further consideration of the unexpected results evidence. Id. IPR2022-00722 Patent 7,041,786 B2 More specifically, the Federal Circuit identified “three reasons” why “the Board’s determination that the[se] experiments compared the claimed invention with the closest prior art lack[ed] adequate explanation” and instructed us to address these issues on remand. See CAFC Dec. 11–13. First, the Court stated we “should determine, based on the evidence put forward by the parties, whether plecanatide exhibits topoisomerism and whether the testing of the challenged claims should encompass both the active and inactive forms.” Id. at 12. $^{3}$ Second, the Court instructed us to reconsider what the closest prior art compound is in view of Patent Owner’s “contention that highly pure A-form uroguanylin could be maintained as a lyophilized powder,” noting that “[i]f it is true . . . that uroguanylin can be maintained and therefore tested in its active A form as a lyophilized powder, that would support that the experiments [which were conducted in solution] did not use the closest prior art.” Id. Third, the Court observed that the Decision did not sufficiently address “whether the experiments submitted by [Patent Owner] actually used” the closest prior art and instructed the Board “to reevaluate the experimental evidence consistent” with the determinations noted above. Id. at 12–13. IPR2022-00722 Patent 7,041,786 B2 On remand, both parties sought authorization to file post-remand briefing addressing the three issues identified in the CAFC Decision. See Ex. 3004, 1–2 (joint email from counsel). $^{4}$ Patent Owner also asked to submit additional evidence regarding the same. Id. Following a telephonic hearing, we authorized post-remand briefing and certain additional evidence from Patent Owner “limited to facts regarding the same experiments and data already of record and presented in Patent Owner’s Response.” Paper 92, 3–5; see also Ex. 3006, 1. We gave Petitioner an opportunity to respond with additional evidence of its own. Paper 92, 5–6. Pursuant to that authorization, Patent Owner filed an opening brief $^{5}$ (Paper 94, “PO Remand Brief”) with additional exhibits, including declarations from Dr. Shailubhai (Ex. 2072) and Dr. Pennington (Ex. 2023). Petitioner filed a responsive brief (Paper 98, “Pet. Remand Resp.”) with additional exhibits, including a declaration from Dr. Peterson (Ex. 1078) and Patent Owner filed a reply (Paper 103, “PO Remand Reply”). Depositions were also taken of each of these witnesses. See Ex. 1079 (Shailubhai); Ex. 1080 (Pennington); Ex. 2077 (Peterson). After considering the parties' arguments and evidence, in view of the guidance in the CAFC Decision, we again find that Petitioner has not shown by a preponderance of the evidence that the challenged claims of the '786 patent are unpatentable
key_sections.challenged_patent:
The '786 patent issued on May 9, 2006, from a utility application filed on March 28, 2002, and claims priority to a provisional application filed on January 17, 2002. Ex. 1001, codes (22), (45), (60). The '786 patent is titled “Guanylate Cyclase Receptor Agonists for the Treatment of Tissue Inflammation and Carcinogenesis.” Ex. 1001, code (54). According to the Specification, [t]he present invention relates to the therapeutic use of guanylate cyclase receptor agonists as a means for enhancing the intracellular production of cGMP. The agonists may be used either alone or in combination with inhibitors of cGMP-specific phosphodiesterase to prevent or treat cancerous, pre-cancerous and metastatic growths, particularly in the gastrointestinal tract and lungs. In addition, the agonists may be used in the treatment of inflammatory disorders such as ulcerative colitis and asthma. ## Id. at 1:14–23. The Specification explains that “[u]roguanylin, guanylin and bacterial ST [i.e., heat-stable enterotoxin] peptides are structurally related peptides that bind to a guanylate cyclase receptor and stimulate intracellular production of cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP).” Ex. 1001, 1:26–29. “This results in the activation of the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR),” which in turn “leads to stimulation of sodium and water secretion into the intestinal lumen." Id. at 1:29–36. "Therefore, by serving as paracrine regulators of CFTR activity, cGMP receptor agonists," such as uroguanylin, guanylin, and bacterial ST peptides, "regulate fluid and electrolyte transport in the GI tract." Id. at 1:36–39. "The present invention is based upon the development of new agonists of guanylate cyclase receptor" that "are analogs of uroguanylin." Ex. 1001, 3:3–6. The Specification describes the sequence for its "most preferred" uroguanylin analog in SEQ ID NO:20, which is reproduced below. Asn $^{1}$ Asp $^{2}$ Glu $^{3}$ Cys $^{4}$ Glu $^{5}$ Leu $^{6}$ Cys $^{7}$ Val $^{8}$ Asn $^{9}$ Val $^{10}$ Ala $^{11}$ Cys $^{12}$ Thr $^{13}$ Gly $^{14}$ Cys $^{15}$ Leu $^{16}$ Id. at 5:7–12. SEQ ID NO:20 provides the primary structure of a 16-amino acid long peptide. The sequence in SEQ ID NO:20 differs from that of the “Parent compound: uroguanylin” (shown in SEQ ID NO: 1) in that it has a “Glu $^{3}$ ,” i.e., a glutamic acid (Glu) at position 3 in the peptide sequence, whereas human uroguanylin has an “Asp $^{3}$ ,” i.e., an aspartic acid (Asp) at that position. $^{6}$ See id. at 11 (Table 2). This is the only difference between the sequence in SEQ ID NO:20 and the naturally–occurring human uroguanylin sequence in SEQ ID NO:1. Id.; see also Ex. 1002 ¶¶ 24–25. According to the Specification, the peptide of SEQ ID NO:20 has been found to have enhanced biological activity as an agonist of cGMP production due to its enhanced binding constant for the guanylate cyclase receptor, and is superior to uroguanylin with regard to temperature and protease stability and with regard to its biological activity at the physiologically favorable pH range (pH 6 to 7) in the large intestine. Ex. 1001, 5:17–23. Table 4 in the Specification shows data from a “T84 cell-based assay for determining the intracellular levels of cGMP.” Ex. 1001, 15:35–36. “Peptide analogs were custom synthesized by Multiple Peptide Systems, San Diego, Calif., and by Princeton Biomolecules, Langhorne, Pa. Biological activity of the synthetic peptides was assayed as previously reported.” Id. at 15:36–40 (citing the Shailubhai publication). $^{7}$ In addition, the ’786 patent states that the “Peptides shown in Table 4 were custom synthesized and purified (>95% purity) using a published procedure.” Id at 15:53–54 (citing Klodt). $^{8}$ Table 4 is reproduced below and shows data for human uroguanylin (SP301) and plecanatide (SP304). TABLE 4
Peptide agonists evaluated for biological activity in the T84 cell bioassay.
SEQ ID NO.*Compound CodecGMP Level** (pmol/well)
1SP301205
6SP302225
7SP303195
20SP304315
14SP3060
4SP3100
21SP316275
\*SEQ ID's for SP301, SP304 and SP316 are the precise amino acid sequences for these analogs as given in the text. \*\*Intracellular cGMP level observed in T84 cells following treatment with 1 micromolar solution of the respective peptide agonist for 30 minutes. The value observed for SP304 was statistically significant with a p > 0.5. "As shown in Table 4 [above], SP304 (SEQ ID NO:20) gave the greatest enhancement of intracellular cGMP of all the" peptides tested. Id. at 15:53–58. ## C. Challenged Claims The Petition challenges claims 1–6. Claims 1–3 and 6 are independent claims. They provide as follows: 1. A peptide consisting of the amino acid sequence of SEQ ID NO:20. 2. A composition in unit dose comprising a guanylate cyclase receptor agonist peptide consisting of the amino acid sequence of SEQ ID NO:20. 3. A composition in unit dose form comprising: a) a guanylate cyclase receptor agonist peptide consisting of the amino acid sequence of SEQ ID NO: 20; and b) at least one compound selected from the group consisting of: a cGMP-dependent phosphodiesterase inhibitor, an anti-inflammatory agent, an antiviral agent and an anticancer agent. 6. A peptide conjugate comprising polyethylene glycol (PEG) attached to a peptide consisting of the amino acid sequence SEQ ID NO:20. Ex. 1001, 37:2–12, 38:7–9. Claims 4 and 5 additionally limit the compositions of either claim 2 or 3 to a “dose form . . . selected from the group consisting of a tablet, a capsule, a solution and an inhalation formulation” (claim 4) and to further comprise “one or more excipients” (claim 5). Id. at 38:1–6. Claim 1 is directed to a peptide
key_sections.asserted_grounds:
Petitioner asserts the following grounds of unpatentability:
Claim(s) Challenged35 U.S.C. §9Reference(s)/Basis

## E. Incorporation of Prior Decision All of Petitioner's asserted grounds of unpatentability rely on the same combination of Currie and Li for the limitations of claim 1 and the similar limitations recited in the other independent claims. In the Decision, we found that, based on the teachings in those references, an ordinarily-skilled artisan would have been motivated both to select human uroguanylin as a lead compound and modify its sequence by making a conservative Glu $^{3}$ substitution for uroguanylin's Asp $^{3}$ . Dec. 15–37. We did not, however, credit Petitioner's other purported rationales for that modification. Id. at 34–37. Those findings were not disturbed on appeal. See CAFC Dec. 6–8 (rejecting Patent Owner's alternative arguments for affirmance). We incorporate by reference the portions of the Decision addressing Petitioner's prima facie showing herein. See Dec. 8–37 (§§ III.A–D and E.i–ii). We consider the findings and explanation therein to the extent it bears on our analysis below
key_sections.analysis: The focus on remand is Patent Owner's unexpected results showing. In the Decision, we found there were three categories of unexpected results data that evidenced non-obviousness: (1) interconversion stability, (2) potency, and (3) binding affinity. $^{15}$ See Dec. 37–59. Categories (1) and (2) are based on the Patent Owner's own data in Table 4 of the Specification and studies described in Exhibits 2027 and 2028. $^{16}$ Id. at 38. Category (3) is based on data that was published in a research article, Liu. $^{17}$ Id. at 56. On remand, Patent Owner continues to assert that these three categories of data demonstrate unexpected results compared to the closest prior art on remand. See PO Remand Br. 2. Petitioner disputes the same. See generally Pet. Remand Resp. (urging “Patent Owner has not met its burden to show its alleged ‘unexpected results’ compared the invention to the closest prior art, or that the unexpected results are commensurate in scope with the claims.”). We begin by addressing the three issues identified by the Federal Circuit. See CAFC Dec. 12–13. After addressing those issues, we assess the specifics of each category of Patent Owner's results as well as Petitioner's argument that those results are not reasonably commensurate with the scope of the claims. Finally, we weigh the record as a whole to reach a conclusion on obviousness. IPR2022-00722 Patent 7,041,786 B2 ## A. Whether plecanatide exhibits topoisomerism The parties agree that plecanatide, like uroguanylin, exhibits topoisomerism, meaning it “exists in, and can interconvert between, two topoisomeric forms, one active and the other inactive.” PO Remand Br. 1, 4 (citing Ex. 2024 ¶¶ 50, 53; Ex. 2040); see also Pet. Remand Resp. 28 (acknowledging same). This means that claim 1, which recites a “peptide consisting of the amino acid sequence of SEQ ID NO:20,” encompasses that peptide regardless of its topoisomeric form. Ex. 1063 ¶ 150 (“Sequence ID No. 20 does not require any particular topoisomer . . . but instead would encompass a composition of peptide consisting of Sequence ID No. 20 that is purely an ‘active’ topoisomer, purely an ‘inactive’ topoisomer, or any mixture of two or more topoisomers sharing Sequence ID No. 20.”). Patent Owner agrees that “Claim 1 encompasses plecanatide in both topoisomeric forms,” but attempts to distinguish claim 2, urging that it “focuses on the active topoisomer of plecanatide by reciting ‘a guanylate cyclase receptor agonist peptide consisting of the amino acid sequence of SEQ ID NO:20.” PO Remand Br. 5 (emphasis in original). Petitioner responds, asserting that “[t]o the extent . . . Patent Owner is now raising claim construction to argue that claim 2 does not stand or fall with claim 1,” something it has never done before, “it is far too late . . . for such gamesmanship.” Pet. Remand Resp. 29. Petitioner further points to evidence suggesting that even the inactive topoisomer is likely to have some agonist activity. Id. at 28 (citing Ex. 1078 ¶¶ 22–23). We agree that it is too late for Patent Owner to argue that claim 2 does not stand or fall with claim 1. As Petitioner points out, “Patent Owner has never separately argued the patentability of claims 2–6” before. Pet. Remand IPR2022-00722 Patent 7,041,786 B2 Resp. 28 n. 16. And even now, Patent Owner does not articulate any argument for treating claim 2 (or any of the other challenged claims) differently from claim 1. PO Remand Br. 5 (asserting only that claim 2 “focuses on plecanatide’s active form”); see generally PO Reply (electing not to respond to Petitioner’s arguments regarding claim 2). But even if we were to consider such an argument, we do not discern any reason to treat claim 2 differently than claim 1 in assessing Patent Owner’s unexpected results showing. It is undisputed that the active topoisomer of plecanatide is a guanylate cyclase receptor agonist peptide and the evidence of record shows such. See infra § III.C.ii–iii (showing plecanatide’s activity and binding affinity). Thus, there is a nexus between the peptide of claim 2 and the alleged unexpected results. Moreover, to the extent Patent Owner contends that claim 2 is limited to only the active topoisomer, we disagree. First, Patent Owner does not cite evidence to support its assertion that the “inactive form of plecanatide is not an agonist for the claimed receptor.” See PO Remand Br. 5. In contrast, Petitioner points to evidence that even the “inactive” topoisomer of uroguanylin shows some guanylate cyclase receptor agonist activity in cGMP assays. Ex. 1078 ¶ 23; Ex. 2010, 239 (Fig. 8); Ex. 2074, 48 (observing that the inactive topoisomer “showed maximal 10%” activity of the active topoisomer). For this reason, we credit the testimony of Petitioner’s expert Dr. Peterson that an ordinarily-skilled artisan would not expect the inactive form of plecanatide to be “wholly inactive” or “devoid of agonist activity.” Ex. 1078 ¶ 23. Second, as Petitioner points out, nothing in the claims or Specification of the '786 patent defines the level of activity required for the peptide to be considered a “guanylate cyclase receptor agonist.” Pet. Remand Resp. 29: Ex. 1078 ¶ 24. Accordingly, even if the ‘inactive” topoisomer exhibits greatly reduced activity relative to the “active” form, we do not read claim 2 to exclude the former by reciting a “guanylate cyclase receptor agonist peptide.” Ex. 1001, 37:4–5. As for the Federal Circuit's related question about whether the testing should encompass both of plecanatide's topoisomers (see CAFC Dec. 12), we note that it likely does even if the parties' evidence does not directly show such. Because plecanatide is subject to topoisomerism it exists as a mixture of those topoisomers when it is in solution that trends to an equilibrium state of the isomeric forms. Ex. 2024 ¶¶ 51, 73. As explained below, the relevant assays are carried out in solution. See infra § III.B. Thus, even when testing plecanatide that had been purified to a h
key_sections.conclusion:
Weighing the record as a whole in view of the findings and analysis above as well as our findings and analysis relating to Petitioner's prima facie showing in the Decision, we conclude that Petitioner has not met its burden to show that claim 1 would have been obvious over Currie and Li. The evidence suggesting that it would have been obvious to make the Glu $^{3}$ substitution because it is a conservative substitution (see Dec. 20–38 (§ III.E.ii)) is substantially outweighed by Patent Owner's comparatively strong evidence demonstrating unexpected results relating to improved interconversion stability, potency, and binding affinity from the Glu $^{3}$ substitution. See Millennium Pharms., Inc. v. Sandoz Inc., 862 F.3d 1356, 1368 (Fed. Cir. 2017) (“Evidence of objective indicia [such as unexpected results] ‘can be the most probative evidence of nonobviousness in the record,’ and . . . enable[s] ‘the court to avert the trap of hindsight.’”) (quoting Leo Pharm. Prods., Ltd. v. Rea, 726 F.3d 1346, 1358 (Fed. Cir. 2013). Indeed, the interconversion and potency categories of Patent Owner's unexpected results showing are particularly strong such that even without the additional results in Liu, we would still reach the same ultimate conclusion on obviousness. Accordingly, Petitioner has not shown by a preponderance of the evidence that claim 1 is unpatentable. ## F. Other Grounds Petitioner's other grounds challenge claims 2–6 and are premised on Petitioner's showing in Ground 1 for the limitations of claim 1. See Pet. 40–53. Petitioner relies on the additional references asserted in these grounds only for their teachings regarding the additional limitations recited in the dependent claims. Id. Because Petitioner has not shown that claim 1 would have been obvious over Currie and Li, the premise for Petitioner's other grounds has not been established. Accordingly, Petitioner has not shown by a preponderance of the evidence that claims 2–6 are unpatentable. ## IV. PATENT OWNER'S MOTION TO EXCLUDE Patent Owner moves to exclude page 86 of Exhibit 1067 (the “American Peptide letter”) as hearsay. Paper 101, 1 (“MTE”). This motion is essentially a continuation of Patent Owner’s earlier motion to exclude Exhibit 1067 (Paper 53), which we granted in part with respect to the materials submitted by the opponent in the EPO opposition. See Dec. 63–72 (referring to these materials as “the Currie submission” and excluding the same as hearsay). The American Peptide letter was apparently among the materials submitted by the EPO opponent. See Ex. 1067, 93 (“Opponent 1 files herewith a letter from the American Peptide Company”). However, at the time of our Decision, we did not discern Petitioner’s reliance on that letter so page 86 of Exhibit 1067 was not included amongst the pages we excluded in the Currie submission. Dec. 63, 69 (excluding “pages 88–98 and 120–127 of Exhibit 1067,” but dismissing the remainder of Patent Owner’s motion as moot). On remand, Petitioner specifically relies on the American Peptide letter. Pet. Remand Resp. 25 (quoting Ex. 1067, 86). Patent Owner sought authorization to file the present motion to exclude, which we allowed. Paper 99. Petitioner opposes that motion. See Paper 102 (“MTE Opp.”). Having considered both sides' arguments, we grant Patent Owner's motion. The American Peptide letter contains out of court statements from a third party submitted by the EPO opponent. The statements in the letter were not made under oath, nor is there evidence that Patent Owner was ever able to question the author of that letter or anyone else with personal knowledge of the statements therein. Petitioner argues that the American Peptide letter is evidence “to doubt that the ‘singular peak’ for uroguanylin in Liu represents the active topoisomer of uroguanylin” because it evidences that American Peptide “provided its customers with the ‘B’ (i.e., inactive) form of uroguanylin at the time.” Pet. Remand Resp. 25. That is, Petitioner relies on the American Peptide letter for the truth of the matters asserted therein. Such use plainly implicates the rule against hearsay and falls squarely within our prior reasoning for excluding the Currie submission portion of Exhibit 1067. We incorporate that analysis herein. See Dec. 61–72. Petitioner asserts that the American Peptide letter is admissible under the residual hearsay exception in FRE 807. MTE Opp. 3–5. We disagree. The American Peptide letter was submitted by the EPO opponent specifically for the purpose of opposing a foreign counterpart to the '786 patent. We see no reason to distinguish it from the other materials previously excluded as hearsay in the Currie submission. Petitioner's assertion that it contains sufficient guarantees of trustworthiness because it was "provided by a neutral third-party" (American Peptide) and "an individual having first- hand knowledge, [American Peptide Company's] VP of sales and marketing" is speculation. See id. at 3–4. Based on the record before us, Patent Owner has never been afforded an opportunity to examine anyone regarding the statements in this letter or even confirm that it is, in fact, a letter from the American Peptide Company. The mere fact that it was submitted in a foreign patent proceeding does not make it any more trustworthy than any other untested assertion offered by an adverse party in a legal proceeding. For these reasons, we grant Patent Owner's motion to exclude page 86 of Exhibit 1067. ## V. CONCLUSION Petitioner has not shown, by a preponderance of the evidence, that claims 1–6 of the '786 patent are unpatentable.

patent_lookup.patent_title: Guanylate cyclase receptor agonists for the treatment of tissue inflammation and carcinogenesis
patent_lookup.abstract: A method of treatment of inflamed, pre-cancerous or cancerous tissue or polyps in a mammalian subject is disclosed. The treatment involves administration of a composition of at least one peptide agonist of a guanylate cyclase receptor and/or other small molecules that enhance intracellular production of cGMP. The at least one peptide agonist of a guanylate cyclase receptor may be administered either alone or in combination with an inhibitor of cGMP-dependent phosphodiesterase. The inhibitor may be a small molecule, peptide, protein or other compound that inhibits the degradation of cGMP. Without requiring a particular mechanism of action, this treatment may restore a healthy balance between proliferation and apoptosis in the subject's population of epithelial cells, and also suppress carcinogenesis. Thus, the method may be used to treat, inter alia, inflammation, including gastrointestinal inflammatory disorders, general organ inflammation and asthma, and carcinogenesis of the lung, gastrointestinal tract, bladder, testis, prostate and pancreas, or polyps.

key_sections.background: Dr. Squatch, LLC (“Petitioner”) filed a Petition requesting inter partes review of claims 1–7 and 9–19 of U.S. Patent No. 10,905,647 B2 (Ex. 1001, “the ’647 patent”). Petition (“Pet.”), Paper 1. The Procter & Gamble Company (“Patent Owner”) filed a Preliminary Response identifying itself as the owner of the ’647 patent. Preliminary Response (“Prelim. Resp.”), Paper 7. We instituted trial on January 29, 2025. Paper 12 (“Inst. Dec.”). During trial, Patent Owner filed a Patent Owner Response. Paper 16 (“PO Resp.”). Petitioner filed a Reply (Paper 20 (“Pet. Reply”)), and Patent Owner filed a Sur-reply (Paper 25 (“PO Sur-Reply”)). We held an oral hearing on October 22, 2025, and a transcript of that hearing is of record. Paper 34 (“Tr.”). We have jurisdiction under 35 U.S.C. § 6(b). After considering the full record developed through trial, we determine that Petitioner has proved by a preponderance of the evidence that the challenged claims are unpatentable. See 35 U.S.C. § 316(e). Our reasoning is explained below, and we issue this Final Written Decision under 35 U.S.C. § 318(a)
key_sections.challenged_patent: The '647 patent is titled “Antiperspirant and Deodorant Compositions.” Ex. 1001, code (54). The '647 patent is directed to antiperspirant and deodorant compositions and methods of making the compositions. Id. at 1:15–16, 10:65. More specifically, the '647 patent is directed to formulating, in response to consumer demands, an aluminum-free, natural fragrance deodorant that provides low irritation, while maintaining sufficient odor protection. Id. at 1:41–43. ## The '647 patent explains that [c]onsumers are also seeking more natural products complete with fragrances that are mostly natural or essential oil based. They are further seeking products with lower irritation than they have experienced with baking soda based products. The challenge with formulating with the natural and essential oil fragrances is that they can be less stable in the presence of heat and extreme pH (either high or low). And products formulated with baking soda, which has a relatively high pH and high water solubility, can increase irritation, even for consumers with moderate sweat rates. Highly water soluble alkaline powders contribute negatively towards natural or essential oil stability as well, especially in hot process[es] needed to melt waxes. And high water soluble powders can also lead to gritty product, as the water soluble powders can agglomerate when exposed to moisture released from powders during the hot batch process. Id. at 1:25–40. The '647 patent further states that the inventors of the present invention have found, surprisingly, that the combination of piroctone olamine and other antimicrobials can provide significant antimicrobial activity against two of the most common underarm odor bacteria C. mucofaciens and S. epidermidis, which results in consumer odor protection on par or greater than some of the commonly used commercial deodorants available today. Ex. 1001, 1:48–55; see id. at 12:24–34 (describing regulatory problems with using a sufficient amount of only one antimicrobial). To maintain the efficacy of piroctone olamine in the presence of iron and other impurities, the '647 patent states that combining chelators and piroctone olamine in an anhydrous formulation or in particular ratios “can provide significantly higher levels of anti-fungal activity than either material alone.” Id. at 1:56–63, 7:48–55 (stating a chelator can reduce the amount of piroctone olamine bonding with the iron impurity). The '647 patent also observes that antimicrobials may be able to reduce microbes on the skin, but not affect microbes within hair follicles that may contribute to malodor. Ex. 1001, 1:64–67. To solve this problem, “[t]he inventors of the present invention have found that piroctone olamine and other antimicrobial powders, if used at a size that can fit into a hair follicle, can deliver surprisingly superior antimicrobial activity.” Id. at 1:67–2:3. “It is believed that antimicrobial powders may provide a better deposition and have more longevity on the skin than antimicrobials delivered in a different form.” Id. at 10:61–63; see id. at 11:1–21. According to the '647 patent, “a deodorant stick having at least about 25% of a liquid triglyceride and structurants that have a melting point of greater than about 50°C. can result in a deodorant stick with a hardness from about 70 mm\*10 to about 140 mm\*10.” Id. at 15:7–10. The '647 patent asserts that “[s]uch a deodorant stick is able to comprise consumerperceived natural ingredients, while offering a pleasant consumer experience in terms of its hardness.” Id. at 15:10–13. The '647 patent describes that the antiperspirant/deodorant compositions “can contain a primary structurant, an antiperspirant active, a perfume, and additional chassis ingredient(s) . . . in the form of a solid stick.” Id. at 14:47–54. The '647 patent defines a “solid structurant” as “any material known or otherwise effective in providing suspending, gelling, viscosifying, solidifying, and/or thickening properties to the composition or which otherwise provide structure to the final product form.” Id. at 15:58–62. ## The '647 patent further describes a deodorant stick as comprising at least 25% of a liquid triglyceride; a primary antimicrobial having a water solubility of at most about 90 g/L at $25^{\circ}$ C; a fragrance composition comprising at least about 50% natural oils, essential oils, or a combination thereof; and a primary structurant with a melting point of at least $50^{\circ}$ C.; said deodorant stick being free of an aluminum salt; and said deodorant stick having a hardness from about 70 mm\*10 to about 140 mm\*10, as measured by penetration with ASTM D-1321 needle. Id. at 2:11–20. ## E. The Challenged Claims Petitioner challenges claims 1–7, 9–13, and 16–19. $^{1}$ Pet. 1. Claims 1, 16, and 18 are independent. Claim 1 includes a hardness limitation that is contested in this inter partes review, but claims 16 and 17 that are subject to the anticipation ground involving Lesniak do not. Therefore, we find that claims 1, 16, and 17 are representative and are reproduced below. 1. A deodorant stick comprising: a. at least 25% by weight liquid triglyceride; b. a primary antimicrobial having a water solubility of at most about 90g/L at $25^{\circ}$ C.; c. a fragrance composition comprising at least about 50% by weight of natural oils, essential oils, or a combination thereof; and d. a primary structurant with a melting point of at least about $50^{\circ}$ C.; and said deodorant stick being free of an aluminum salt; and said deodorant stick having a hardness from about 70 mm\*10 to about 140 mm\*10, as measured by penetration with ASTM D-1321 needle. Ex. 1001, 27:20–33. 16. A deodorant stick comprising: a. at least 60% by weight liquid triglyceride; b. at least one antimicrobial; c. a fragrance composition comprising at least about 50% by weight of natural oils, essential oils, or a combination thereof; and said deodorant stick being free of an aluminum salt. 17. The deodorant stick

## H. Claim Construction The challenged claims “should be read in light of the Specification, as it would be interpreted by one of ordinary skill in the art.” In re Suitco Surface, Inc., 603 F.3d 1255, 1260 (Fed. Cir. 2010). Thus, we generally give claim terms their ordinary and customary meaning. See In re Translogic Tech., Inc., 504 F.3d 1249, 1257 (Fed. Cir. 2007) (“The ordinary and customary meaning is the meaning that the term would have to a person of ordinary skill in the art in question.” (internal quotation marks omitted)); see also 37 C.F.R. § 42.100(b) (stating that claims are construed in IPRs according to the same standard as used in federal court). Petitioner does not argue that any claim terms require express construction. See generally Pet. Patent Owner asserts that the preamble of independent claim 1 reciting “a deodorant stick” is limiting” as it provides antecedent basis for certain other claim elements, specifically, for the recitation of “said deodorant stick being free of an aluminum salt” and “said deodorant stick” having a particular hardness range. PO Resp. 8–9. Patent Owner also points to dependent claims 5–8 as relying on the preamble reciting “a deodorant stick” as antecedent basis for claim elements. Id. at 10. We agree. Petitioner does not provide argument to the contrary. See, e.g., Pet. 24 (arguing that Lesniak discloses a deodorant stick in the form of an antiperspirant stick). We determine that no express construction of any claim term is necessary to determine whether to institute inter partes review. Nidec Motor Corp. v. Zhongshan Broad Ocean Motor Co., 868 F.3d 1013, 1017 (Fed. Cir. 2017) (“[W]e need only construe terms ‘that are in controversy, and only to the extent necessary to resolve the controversy.’” (quoting Vivid Techs., Inc. v. Am. Sci. & Eng’g, Inc., 200 F.3d 795, 803 (Fed. Cir. 1999))). To the extent further discussion of the meaning any claim term is necessary to our decision, we provide that discussion below in our analysis of the asserted grounds of unpatentability. ## I. Level of Ordinary Skill in the Art Petitioner proposes that a person of ordinary skill in the art (“POSA” or “POSITA”) at the time of the invention “would have (1) at least a B.S. or equivalent degree; and (2) at least two years of experience in cosmetics formulations.” Pet. 24 (citing Ex. 1002 ¶¶ 27–28). Patent Owner does not dispute Petitioner’s proposal about the POSA’s qualifications. See generally PO Resp. We adopt and apply Petitioner's proposed definition of a POSA, which is consistent with the level of skill reflected in the asserted prior art
key_sections.analysis: ## A. Introduction “In an inter partes review, the petitioner has the burden from the onset to show with particularity why the patent it challenges is unpatentable.” Harmonic Inc. v. Avid Tech., Inc., 815 F.3d 1356, 1363 (Fed. Cir. 2016) (citing 35 U.S.C. § 312(a)(3) (requiring inter partes review petitions to identify “with particularity . . . the evidence that supports the grounds for the challenge to each claim”)). This burden of persuasion never shifts to the patent owner. See Dynamic Drinkware, LLC v. Nat’l Graphics, Inc., 800 F.3d 1375, 1378 (Fed. Cir. 2015). Moreover, a petitioner should not “place the burden on [the Board] to sift through information presented by the Petitioners, determine where each element [of the challenged claims] is found in [the cited references], and identify any differences between the claimed subject matter and the teachings of [the cited references.]” Google Inc. v. EveryMD.com LLC, IPR2014-00347, Paper 9 at 25 (PTAB May 22, 2014). ## 1. Anticipation To show anticipation under § 102, each and every claim element, arranged as in the claim, must be found in a single prior art reference. Net MoneyIN, Inc. v. VeriSign, Inc., 545 F.3d 1359 (Fed. Cir. 2008). The prior art need not, however, use the same words as the claims in order to find anticipation. In re Gleave, 560 F.3d 1331, 1334 (Fed. Cir. 2009). It is also permissible to take into account not only the literal teachings of the prior art reference, but also the inferences an ordinarily skilled person would draw from the reference. Eli Lilly and Co. v. Los Angeles Biomedical Res. Inst. at Harbor-UCLA Med. Ctr., 849 F.3d 1073, 1074–75 (Fed. Cir. 2017); In re Preda, 401 F.2d 825, 826 (CCPA 1968). A reference may also anticipate a claim even if it does not expressly teach all the limitations arranged or combined as in the claim, “if a person of skill in the art, reading the reference, would ‘at once envisage’ the claimed arrangement or combination.” Microsoft Corp. v. Biscotti, Inc., 878 F.3d 1052, 1068 (Fed. Cir. 2017) (quoting Kennametal, Inc. v. Ingersoll Cutting Tool Co., 780 F.3d IPR2024-01173 Patent 10,905,647 B2 1376, 1381 (Fed. Cir. 2015)). ## 2. Obviousness A claim is unpatentable under 35 U.S.C. § 103 if the differences between the claimed invention and the prior art are such that the claimed invention as a whole would have been obvious before the effective filing date of the claimed invention to a person having ordinary skill in the art to which the claimed invention pertains. See KSR Int'l Co. v. Teleflex Inc., 550 U.S. 398, 406 (2007). The question of obviousness is resolved on the basis of underlying factual determinations including: (1) the scope and content of the prior art; (2) any differences between the claimed subject matter and the prior art; (3) the level of ordinary skill in the art; and (4) objective evidence of nonobviousness, if any. Graham v. John Deere Co., 383 U.S. 1, 17–18 (1966). In analyzing the obviousness of a combination of prior art elements, it can be important to identify a reason that would have prompted one of skill in the art “to combine . . . known elements in the fashion claimed by the patent at issue.” KSR, 550 U.S. at 418. A precise teaching directed to the specific subject matter of a challenged claim is not necessary to establish obviousness. Id. Rather, “any need or problem known in the field of endeavor at the time of invention and addressed by the patent can provide a reason for combining the elements in the manner claimed.” Id. at 420. Accordingly, a party that petitions the Board for a determination of unpatentability based on obviousness must show that “a skilled artisan would have been motivated to combine the teachings of the prior art references to achieve the claimed invention, and that the skilled artisan would have had a reasonable expectation of success in doing so.” In re Magnum Oil Tools Int’l, Ltd., 829 F.3d 1364, 1381 (Fed. Cir. 2016) (internal quotation marks omitted). “Both the suggestion and the expectation of success must be founded in the prior art, not in the applicant’s disclosure.” In re Dow Chemical Co., 837 F.2d 469, 473 (Fed. Cir. 1988). An obviousness analysis “need not seek out precise teachings directed to the specific subject matter of the challenged claim, for a court can take account of the inferences and creative steps that a person of ordinary skill in the art would employ.” KSR, 550 U.S. at 418; see In re Translogic Tech, Inc., 504 F.3d 1249, 1259 (Fed. Cir. 2007). In KSR, the Supreme Court also stated that an invention may be found obvious if trying a course of conduct would have been obvious to a POSITA: When there is a design need or market pressure to solve a problem and there are a finite number of identified, predictable solutions, a person of ordinary skill has good reason to pursue the known options within his or her technical grasp. If this leads to the anticipated success, it is likely the product not of innovation but of ordinary skill and common sense. In that instance the fact that a combination was obvious to try might show that it was obvious under § 103. 550 U.S. at 421. “KSR affirmed the logical inverse of this statement by stating that § 103 bars patentability unless ‘the improvement is more than the predictable use of prior art elements according to their established functions.’” In re Kubin, 561 F.3d 1351, 1359–60 (Fed. Cir. 2009) (citing KSR, 550 U.S. at 417). We analyze the asserted grounds of unpatentability in accordance with these principles to determine whether Petitioner has met its burden to show by a preponderance of the evidence that the challenged claims are unpatentable. IPR2024-01173 Patent 10,905,647 B2 ## B. Summary of the Cited Prior Art 1. Lesniak (Ex. 1003) Lesniak is titled “NON-GREASY PERSONAL CARE COMPOSITIONS.” Ex. 1003, code (54). Lesniak provides an anhydrous personal care composition, comprising (a) at least one ester, wherein said at least one ester is selected from one or more
key_sections.conclusion: For the foregoing reasons, we determine that Petitioner has shown a by a preponderance of the evidence that claims 1–7, 9–13, and 16–19 of the '647 patent are unpatentable. ## D. The Native Grounds (Grounds 5–7) Petitioner presents three grounds that rely on Native to teach the majority of the limitations of the asserted claims in Grounds 5 through 7. See Pet. 41–66. For Ground 5, Petitioner asserts that claims 16 and 17 of the '647 patent would have been obvious over Native and Bianchi '254. Pet. 45–53. For Ground 6, Petitioner asserts that claims 1, 2, 4–7, 9–12, 18, and 19 would have been obvious over Native in view of Bianchi '254 and Easy Homemade. Pet. 53–63. For Ground 7, Petitioner asserts that claims 3 and 13 would have been obvious over Native, Bianchi '254, Easy Homemade, and Nabavi. Pet. 63–66. Petitioner provides detailed claim charts for each of these grounds explaining how each claim limitation is disclosed in the asserted references and how its assertions are supported by the testimony of Dr. Michniak-Kohn. See Pet. 45–53 (Ground 5), 53–63 (Ground 6), Pet. 53–63 (Ground 7); Ex. 1002 ¶¶ 119–188. ## 1. Ground 5 – Native and Bianchi '254 ## a) Petitioner's Contentions Petitioner states that “Native discloses features of deodorant stick compositions claimed in the ’647 Patent . . . .” Pet. 46. Specifically, Petitioner states: Native sets forth the ingredients in a natural, aluminum-free deodorant stick that was being marketed and sold in at least 2016. The deodorant stick composition is comprised of caprylic-capric triglyceride, baking soda, stearyl alcohol, hydrogenated castor oil, polyglycerol-3 beeswax, and jojoba esters. Aluminum salts, and synthetic fragrances are not included in the composition. As a POSA would have understood, Native lists its deodorant stick ingredients in order form greatest to least concentration; however, it does not expressly teach the percentage of each ingredient used, including the percentage of liquid triglyceride (i.e., carrier oil). In the absence of express teachings on these points, a POSA would have looked to other publications in the field with suitable weight percentage examples[, like Bianchi '254]. Pet. 46–47; see also id. at 49–63 (claim chart showing where limitations of claims 16 and 17 are taught in Native and Bianchi '254). Petitioner relies on Bianchi '254 to teach the appropriate concentrations of the ingredients of the deodorant stick such as the percentages of liquid triglyceride, a carrier oil. See id. at 46–47. In providing support for the teaching of the limitation “at least 60% by weight liquid triglyceride” in claim 16, Petitioner provides the following explanation from the teachings of Bianchi '254. Bianchi '254 describes an antiperspirant stick with 40-80% oil. In Bianchi '254, part of the oil carrier (20-40% of the total) is a natural oil such as a liquid triglyceride. Bianchi '254 also describes that the antiperspirant stick further comprised 20-40% of silicone oil. A POSA would have understood that silicone oil and liquid triglyceride both functioned as “carrier oils” that, among other things, solubilize and disperse other ingredients, and therefore would have been motivated to use even more liquid triglyceride (up to 80%) when formulating a natural deodorant stick (like in Native) that did not include, e.g., 20-40% silicone oil, to meet what the '647 Patent admits was an existing consumer preference for “more natural products.” Thus, a POSA would have been motivated to use at least about 60% of a liquid triglyceride in a deodorant stick, and would have had a reasonable expectation of success in doing so, inasmuch as (i) Bianchi '254 already suggests a 40% amount, and (ii) a POSA would have, based on Bianchi '254's overall oil teachings, used even more of the liquid triglyceride (up to 80%) when making a stick with only that oil carrier (as taught by Native). The overlap between the claimed liquid triglyceride range and the ranges in the prior art renders the claim presumptively obvious. The '647 Patent discloses nothing critical or unexpected regarding the claimed range relative to the prior art ranges. Pet. 50 (citing Ex. 1001, 25–26; Ex. 1002 ¶¶ 128–132; Ex. 1008 ¶¶ 15–17, 23, 50, claims 17 and 18). As for claim 17's further requirement that the deodorant stick of claim 16 further comprise “a primary structurant having a melting point from about 50°C. to about 80°C.,” Petitioner relies on Native’s teaching of using stearyl alcohol that has the claimed melting point range as the primary structurant in Native’s deodorant stick because it is listed first in the ingredient list. See Pet. 52 (citing Ex. 1001, 15:51–54; Ex. 1008, Tables 1, 5). Petitioner strengthens this teaching by relying further on Bianchi ’254’s teaching that: Singles out stearyl alcohol as a “widely used” structurant of particular interest” for antiperspirants. Bianchi ’254 describes antiperspirant formulations comprising 1-25% of a fatty alcohol, such as stearyl alcohol, as the structurant present in the greatest amount. Thus a POSA would have been motivated to use a primary structurant with a melting point of from about $50^{\circ}$ C. to about $80^{\circ}$ C., such as stearyl alcohol, in a deodorant stick, inasmuch as both Native and Bianchi '254 describe doing so. Pet. 52–53 (citing Ex. 1008 ¶¶ 1, 18, 19, 58, 131 (showing formulations with 18% stearyl alcohol as primary structurant and 3.5% castor wax as secondary structurant)). IPR2024-01173 Patent 10,905,647 B2 ## b) Patent Owner's Contentions Patent Owner provides three reasons why the above-described combination of Native and Bianchi '254 fails for Ground 5. First, Patent Owner asserts that Native's deodorant stick was "a known leader in the field," and the Native reference shows nothing but positive reviews for Native's deodorant stick. PO Resp. 50–51. Consequently, Patent Owner asserts, "[w]ith this level of consumer acceptance and praise, and no identified issues or concerns, there was
key_sections.order: In consideration of the foregoing, it is ORDERED that claims 1–7, 9–13, 16–19 of the '647 patent have been shown to be unpatentable; and FURTHER ORDERED that, because this is a Final Written Decision, parties to this proceeding seeking judicial review of our decision must comply with the notice and service requirements of 37 C.F.R. § 90.2. key_sections.background: Dr. Squatch, LLC (“Petitioner”) filed a Petition requesting inter partes review of claims 1–8 and 10–23 of U.S. Patent No. 11,497,706 B2 (Ex. 1001, “the ’706 patent”). Petition (“Pet.”), Paper 1. The Procter & Gamble Company (“Patent Owner”) filed a Preliminary Response identifying itself as the owner of the ’706 patent. Preliminary Response (“Prelim. Resp.”), Paper 8. We instituted trial on January 29, 2025. Paper 3 (“Inst. Dec.”). During trial, Patent Owner filed a Patent Owner Response. Paper 17 (“PO Resp.”). Petitioner filed a Reply (Paper 21 (“Pet. Reply”)), and Patent Owner filed a Sur-reply (Paper 26 (“PO Sur-Reply”)). We held an oral hearing on October 22, 2025, and a transcript of that hearing is of record. Paper 35 (“Tr.”). We have jurisdiction under 35 U.S.C. § 6(b). After considering the full record developed through trial, we determine that Petitioner has proved by a preponderance of the evidence that the challenged claims are unpatentable. See 35 U.S.C. § 316(e). Our reasoning is explained below, and we issue this Final Written Decision under 35 U.S.C. § 318(a)
key_sections.challenged_patent: The '706 patent is titled “Antiperspirant and Deodorant Compositions.” Ex. 1001, code (54). The '706 patent is directed to antiperspirant and deodorant compositions and methods of making the compositions. Id. at 1:16–17. More specifically, the '706 patent is directed to formulating, in response to consumer demands, an aluminum-free, natural fragrance deodorant that provides low irritation, but maintaining sufficient odor protection. Id. at 1:42–44. ## The '706 patent explains that [c]onsumers are also seeking more natural products complete with fragrances that are mostly natural or essential oil based. They are further seeking products with lower irritation than they have experienced with baking soda based products. The challenge with formulating with the natural and essential oil fragrances is that they can be less stable in the presence of heat and extreme pH (either high or low). And products formulated with baking soda, which has a relatively high pH and high water solubility, can increase irritation, even for consumers with moderate sweat rates. Highly water soluble alkaline powders contribute negatively towards natural or essential oil stability as well, especially in hot process needed to melt waxes. And high water soluble powders can agglomerate when exposed to moisture released from powders during the hot batch process. ## Id. at 1:26–41. The '706 patent further states that the inventors of the present invention have found, surprisingly, that the combination of piroctone olamine and other antimicrobials can provide significant antimicrobial activity against two of the most common underarm odor bacteria C. mucofaciens and S. epidermidis, which results in consumer odor protection on par or greater than some of the commonly used commercial deodorants available today. Ex. 1001, 1:49–56; see id. at 12:21–31 (describing regulatory problems with using a sufficient amount of only one antimicrobial). To maintain the efficacy of piroctone olamine in the presence of iron and other impurities, the '706 patent states that combining chelators and piroctone olamine in an anhydrous formulation or in particular ratios “can provide significantly higher levels of anti-fungal activity than either material alone.” Id. at 1:57–64, 7:48–55 (stating a chelator can reduce the amount of piroctone olamine bonding with the iron impurity). The ’706 patent also observes that antimicrobials may be able to reduce microbes on the skin, but not effect microbes within hair follicles that may contribute to malodor. Ex. 1001, 1:65–2:1. To solve this problem, “[t]he inventors of the present invention have found that piroctone olamine and other antimicrobial powders, if used at a size that can fit into a hair follicle, can deliver surprisingly superior antimicrobial activity.” Id. at 1:65–2:4. “It is believed that antimicrobial powders may provide a better deposition and have more longevity on the skin than antimicrobials delivered in a different form.” Id. at 10:58–60; see id. at 10:65–11:18. According to the '706 patent, “a deodorant stick having at least about 25% of a liquid triglyceride and structurants that have a melting point of greater than about 50°C. can result in a deodorant stick with a hardness from about 70 mm\*10 to about 140 mm\*10.” Id. at 14:48–51. The '706 patent asserts that “[s]uch a deodorant stick is able to comprise consumerperceived natural ingredients, while offering a pleasant consumer experience in terms of its hardness.” Id. at 14:51–54. The '706 patent describes that the antiperspirant/deodorant compositions "can contain a primary structurant, an antiperspirant active, a perfume, and additional chassis ingredient(s) . . . in the form of a solid stick." Id. at 13:64–14:31. The '706 patent defines a "solid structurant" as "any material known or otherwise effective in providing suspending, gelling, viscosifying, solidifying, and/or thickening properties to the composition or which otherwise provide structure to the final product form." Id. at 15:32– ## 36. The '706 patent describes a deodorant stick as comprising at least 25% of a liquid triglyceride; a primary antimicrobial having a water solubility of at most about 90 g/L at $25^{\circ}$ C; a fragrance composition comprising at least about 50% natural oils, essential oils, or a combination thereof; and a primary structurant with a melting point pof at least $50^{\circ}$ C.; said deodorant stick being free of an aluminum salt; and said deodorant stick having a hardness from about 70 mm\*10 to about 140 mm\*10, as measured by penetration with ASTM D-1321 needle. Id., 2:12–21. ## E. The Challenged Claims Petitioner challenges claims 1–8 and 10–23. Pet. 1. Claims 1, 16, and 20 are independent. Claim 1 is representative and is reproduced below: 1. A deodorant stick comprising: at least 25% by weight liquid triglyceride and a primary antimicrobial said deodorant stick being free of an aluminum salt. Ex. 1001, 26:57–60. ## F. Evidence Petitioner relies upon information that includes the following. Ex. 1003, Lesniak et al., US 11,433,018 B2, issued September 6, 2022 (“Lesniak”). Ex. 1004, Phinney et al., US 9,314,412 B2, issued April 19, 2016 (“Phinney”). Ex. 1005, John Henderson Lamb, M.D., Sodium Bicarbonate: An Excellent Deodorant, THE JOURNAL OF INVESTIGATIVE DERMATOLOGY 7(3) 131–33 (1946) (“Lamb”). Ex. 1006, Bianchi et al., US 6,048,518 B2, issued April 11, 2000 (“Bianchi ’518”). Ex. 1007, Wayback Machine archives, captured May 26, 2016, November 23, 2016, and November 1, 2015 respectively for: https://www.nativecos.com, https://www.nativecos.com/product/deodorant/, and https://www.nativecos.com (“Native”). Ex. 1008, Bianchi, US 2007/0166254 A1, published July 19, 2007 (“Bianchi ’254”). Ex. 1009, Wayback Machine archive, captured January 26, 2017, for http://soapdelinews.com/2016/11/easy-homemade-deodorantrecipe.html (“Easy Homemade”). Ex. 1056, Sturgis et al., US 2019/0000734 A1, published Jan. 3, 2019 (“Sturgis). Ex. 1057, Anconi et al

## H. Claim Construction The challenged claims should be read in light of the Specification, as it would be interpreted by one of ordinary skill in the art. In re Suitco Surface, Inc., 603 F.3d 1255, 1260 (Fed. Cir. 2010). Thus, we generally give claim terms their ordinary and customary meaning. See In re Translogic Tech., Inc., 504 F.3d 1249, 1257 (Fed. Cir. 2007) (“The ordinary and customary meaning is the meaning that the term would have to a person of ordinary skill in the art in question.” (internal quotation marks omitted)); see also 37 C.F.R. § 42.100(b) (stating that claims are construed in IPRs according to the same standard as used in federal court). Petitioner does not argue that any claim terms require construction. See generally Pet. Patent Owner asserts that the preamble of independent claim 1, 16, and 20 reciting “a deodorant stick” is limiting” as it provides antecedent basis for the recitation of “said deodorant stick being free of an aluminum salt” for each independent claim. PO Resp. 7–10. Patent Owner also points to dependent claims 6–9 as relying on the preamble reciting “a deodorant stick” as antecedent basis for claim elements. Id. at 9. We agree. Petitioner does not provide argument to the contrary. See, e.g., Pet. 19 (arguing that Lesniak discloses a deodorant stick in the form of an antiperspirant stick). We determine that no express construction of any claim term is necessary to determine whether to institute inter partes review. Nidec Motor Corp. v. Zhongshan Broad Ocean Motor Co., 868 F.3d 1013, 1017 (Fed. Cir. 2017) (“[W]e need only construe terms ‘that are in controversy, and only to the extent necessary to resolve the controversy.’” (quoting Vivid Techs., Inc. v. Am. Sci. & Eng’g, Inc., 200 F.3d 795, 803 (Fed. Cir. 1999))). To the extent further discussion of the meaning any claim term is necessary to our decision, we provide that discussion below in our analysis of the asserted grounds of unpatentability. IPR2024-01174 Patent 11,497,706 B2 ## I. Level of Ordinary Skill in the Art Petitioner proposes that a person of ordinary skill in the art (“POSA” or “POSITA”) at the time of the invention “would have (1) at least a B.S. or equivalent degree; and (2) at least two years of experience in cosmetics formulations.” Pet. 17 (citing Ex. 1002 ¶¶ 27–29). Patent Owner does not dispute Petitioner’s proposal about the POSA’s qualifications at this stage of the proceeding. PO Resp. 10. For this Decision, we adopt and apply Petitioner's proposal above, which does not appear to be inconsistent with the level of skill reflected in the asserted prior art
key_sections.analysis: ## A. Introduction “In an inter partes review, the petitioner has the burden from the onset to show with particularity why the patent it challenges is unpatentable.” Harmonic Inc. v. Avid Tech., Inc., 815 F.3d 1356, 1363 (Fed. Cir. 2016) (citing 35 U.S.C. § 312(a)(3) (requiring inter partes review petitions to identify “with particularity . . . the evidence that supports the grounds for the challenge to each claim”)). This burden of persuasion never shifts to the patent owner. See Dynamic Drinkware, LLC v. Nat’l Graphics, Inc., 800 F.3d 1375, 1378 (Fed. Cir. 2015). Moreover, a petitioner should not “place the burden on [the Board] to sift through information presented by the Petitioners, determine where each element [of the challenged claims] is found in [the cited references], and identify any differences between the claimed subject matter and the teachings of [the cited references.]” Google Inc. v. EveryMD.com LLC, IPR2014-00347, Paper 9 at 25 (PTAB May 22, 2014). IPR2024-01174 Patent 11,497,706 B2 To show anticipation under § 102, each and every claim element, arranged as in the claim, must be found in a single prior art reference. Net MoneyIN, Inc. v. VeriSign, Inc., 545 F.3d 1359 (Fed. Cir. 2008). The prior art need not, however, use the same words as the claims in order to find anticipation. In re Gleave, 560 F.3d 1331, 1334 (Fed. Cir. 2009). It is also permissible to take into account not only the literal teachings of the prior art reference, but also the inferences an ordinarily skilled person would draw from the reference. Eli Lilly and Co. v. Los Angeles Biomedical Res. Inst. at Harbor-UCLA Med. Ctr., 849 F.3d 1073, 1074–75 (Fed. Cir. 2017); In re Preda, 401 F.2d 825, 826 (CCPA 1968). A reference may also anticipate a claim even if it does not expressly teach all the limitations arranged or combined as in the claim, “if a person of skill in the art, reading the reference, would ‘at once envisage’ the claimed arrangement or combination.” Microsoft Corp. v. Biscotti, Inc., 878 F.3d 1052, 1068 (Fed. Cir. 2017) (quoting Kennametal, Inc. v. Ingersoll Cutting Tool Co., 780 F.3d 1376, 1381 (Fed. Cir. 2015)). A claim is unpatentable under 35 U.S.C. § 103 if the differences between the claimed invention and the prior art are such that the claimed invention as a whole would have been obvious before the effective filing date of the claimed invention to a person having ordinary skill in the art to which the claimed invention pertains. See KSR Int'l Co. v. Teleflex Inc., 550 U.S. 398, 406 (2007). The question of obviousness is resolved on the basis of underlying factual determinations including: (1) the scope and content of the prior art; (2) any differences between the claimed subject matter and the prior art; (3) the level of ordinary skill in the art; and (4) objective evidence of nonobviousness, if any. Graham v. John Deere Co., 383 U.S. 1, 17–18 (1966). In analyzing the obviousness of a combination of prior art elements, it can be important to identify a reason that would have prompted one of skill in the art “to combine . . . known elements in the fashion claimed by the patent at issue.” KSR, 550 U.S. at 418. A precise teaching directed to the specific subject matter of a challenged claim is not necessary to establish obviousness. Id. Rather, “any need or problem known in the field of endeavor at the time of invention and addressed by the patent can provide a reason for combining the elements in the manner claimed.” Id. at 420. Accordingly, a party that petitions the Board for a determination of unpatentability based on obviousness must show that “a skilled artisan would have been motivated to combine the teachings of the prior art references to achieve the claimed invention, and that the skilled artisan would have had a reasonable expectation of success in doing so.” In re Magnum Oil Tools Int’l, Ltd., 829 F.3d 1364, 1381 (Fed. Cir. 2016) (internal quotation marks omitted). “Both the suggestion and the expectation of success must be founded in the prior art, not in the applicant’s disclosure.” In re Dow Chemical Co., 837 F.2d 469, 473 (Fed. Cir. 1988). An obviousness analysis “need not seek out precise teachings directed to the specific subject matter of the challenged claim, for a court can take account of the inferences and creative steps that a person of ordinary skill in the art would employ.” KSR, 550 U.S. at 418; see In re Translogic Tech, Inc., 504 F.3d 1249, 1259 (Fed. Cir. 2007). In KSR, the Supreme Court also stated that an invention may be found obvious if trying a course of conduct would have been obvious to a POSITA: When there is a design need or market pressure to solve a problem and there are a finite number of identified, predictable solutions, a person of ordinary skill has good reason to pursue the known options within his or her technical grasp. If this leads to the anticipated success, it is likely the product not of innovation but of ordinary skill and common sense. In that instance the fact that a combination was obvious to try might show that it was obvious under § 103. 550 U.S. at 421. “KSR affirmed the logical inverse of this statement by stating that § 103 bars patentability unless ‘the improvement is more than the predictable use of prior art elements according to their established functions.’” In re Kubin, 561 F.3d 1351, 1359–60 (Fed. Cir. 2009) (citing KSR, 550 U.S. at 417). We analyze the asserted grounds of unpatentability in accordance with these principles to determine whether Petitioner has met its burden to establish a reasonable likelihood of success at trial. ## B. Summary of the Cited Prior Art 1. Lesniak (Ex. 1003) Lesniak is titled “NON-GREASY PERSONAL CARE COMPOSITIONS.” Ex. 1003, code (54). Lesniak provides an anhydrous personal care composition, comprising (a) at least one ester, wherein said at least one ester is selected from one or more of the following groups: (i) triglycerides, (ii) diglycerides, (iii) monoglycerides, (iv) monoesters o
key_sections.conclusion: Based on the fully developed trial record, Petitioner has demonstrated by a preponderance of the evidence that claims 1–8 and 10–23 of the '706 patent are unpatentable. Patent 11,497,706 B2 In summary

key_sections.introduction: Exact Sciences Corporation (“Patent Owner”) is the owner of U.S. Patent 11,970,746 B2 (Ex. 1001, “the ’746 patent”). Paper 4, 2. Geneoscopy, Inc. (“Petitioner”) filed a Petition requesting an inter partes review of claims 1–4 and 12–19 of the ’746 patent. Paper 1 (“Pet.”). Paper 1. Patent Owner’s Preliminary Response identified that claims 1 and 2 of the ’746 patent had been disclaimed. Paper 9 (“Prelim. Resp.”), 2; Ex. 2041 (disclaimer). Therefore, only claims 3, 4, and 12–19 of the ’746 Patent are at issue in this inter partes review, which we instituted on February 14, 2024. Paper 10 (“Institution Decision” or “DI”). On May 30, 2025, Patent Owner filed a Response to the Petition (Paper 19, “Response” or “Resp.”), which was followed by Petitioner’s Reply (Paper 22, “Reply”), which was followed by Patent Owner’s Sur-reply (Paper 30, “Sur-reply”). Oral argument was heard at a hearing held on November 20, 2025, and a transcript of the hearing is of record. See Paper 38 (“Hr’g Tr.”). Petitioner bears the burden of proving unpatentability of the challenged claims and this ultimate burden of persuasion never shifts to Patent Owner. Dynamic Drinkware, LLC v. Nat'l Graphics, Inc., 800 F.3d 1375, 1378 (Fed. Cir. 2015). As we address the parties' positions, it is always in the context of this burden. To prevail, Petitioner must prove unpatentability by a preponderance of the evidence. See 35 U.S.C. § 316(e) (2018); 37 C.F.R. § 42.1(d) (2023). After considering the parties' arguments and supporting evidence, as discussed in detail below, we conclude that Petitioner proves by a preponderance of the evidence that the challenged claims of the '746 patent are unpatentable. IPR2024-01330 Patent 11,970,746 B2
key_sections.challenged_patent: The '746 patent, titled "Fecal Sample Processing and Analysis Comprising Detection of Blood," $^{2}$ was filed as U.S. Application 18/179,945 on March 7, 2023, and claims priority to a series of continuation applications, including U.S. Application 17/936,335 ("the '335 Application"), and U.S. Provisional Application 61/149,581 ("the '581 "Provisional"), which was filed on February 3, 2009. Ex. 1001, codes (54), (21), (22), (63), (60), 1:8–19. Thus, the earliest possible effective filing date of the '746 patent is February 3, 2009. There is no dispute in this proceeding that the '746 patent is entitled to this priority date and we apply this priority date in our analysis here. The '746 patent relates to methods and kits for analysis of fecal samples. Id. at 1:31–32. Its Abstract states that the invention relates to: A method of processing a fecal sample from a human subject comprising combining a first portion of a collected fecal sample with a stabilizing buffer, combining a second portion of the sample with a solution that prevents denaturation or degradation of blood proteins found in a fecal sample. Embodiments comprise testing nucleic acid extracted from the first portion of the fecal sample for an amount of a human nucleic acid, and testing the second portion of the fecal sample for the presence of human blood. Id. at Abstr. According to the Specification, colorectal cancer (“CRC”) is a leading cause of cancer-related deaths worldwide. Id. at 1:41–43. Mass screening of asymptomatic patients is the cornerstone for detecting and eliminating precursor lesions to reduce the risk of CRC. Id. at 1:51–55. Colonoscopy is the primary screening test for CRC because of its high sensitivity and specificity and the ability to contemporaneously remove polyps if found. Id. at 1:65–2:1. That procedure, however, is invasive, costly, and has certain risks, such as infection and perforation of the bowel. Id. at 2:1–3. Fecal occult blood testing (“FOBT”), which tests for blood in the stool as a CRC screen, is commonly used, and is less invasive and expensive than colonoscopy. Id. at 2:4–12. But, because occult blood in stool can be indicative of different gastrointestinal disorders, further testing is necessary to confirm CRC. Id. There are two types of FOBT: guaiac FOBT (“gFOBT”), which detects peroxidase activity of hemoglobin in fecal blood, and immunochemical FOBT (“iFOBT” or “FIT”), which uses anti-human hemoglobin (Hb) antibodies to detect fecal blood. Id. at 2:13–34. Although the immunochemical procedure is comparatively more complicated and expensive, iFOBT is more sensitive than gFOBT. Id. at 2:25–40. The Specification explains that developments in testing also look specifically for mutations in DNA that are characteristic of colorectal neoplasia, which are detectable in exfoliated epithelial cells in stool. Id. at 2:44–47. The Specification explains that increased DNA methylation is an epigenetic alteration that is common in human cancers. Id. at 3:5–7. Aberrantly methylated DNA has also been proposed as a potential tumor marker for CRC detection. Id. at 3:7–9. The '746 patent explains that, although combined assays for detecting CRC have been described, their approaches target either multiple protein markers or multiple DNA alterations. Id. at 3:41–43. According to the Specification, “[t]o date, immunochemical tests and DNA tests for CRC detection have been evaluated and compared on a separate basis only.” Id. at 3:43–45. The '746 patent states that the invention "aims to improve the positive and negative predictive value and also the sensitivity and specificity of detection of colorectal cancer through non-invasive means." Id. at 6:52–55. Accordingly, the invention is based on a combination of tests for detecting proteins and epigenetic modification markers in the same fecal sample. Id. at 6:59–62. The '746 patent concludes with 19 claims. Id. at 45:56–48:36. Petitioner challenges claims 3, 4, and 12–19 of the '746 patent (as noted above, claims 1 and 2 are disclaimed). Claim 3 is illustrative of the challenged claims; it depends from the sole independent claim 1, which was disclaimed, but claim 1's recited subject matter remains incorporated into the dependent claims. Claims 1 and 3 are reproduced below: 1. A method of processing a freshly-collected fecal sample without freezing, the method comprising: a) collecting a fecal sample from a human subject, wherein the fecal sample is collected at home by the human subject; b) in a sealable vessel, combining a first portion of the fecal sample with a stabilizing buffer, and sealing the sealable vessel; and c) in a sealable container, combining the second portion of the fecal sample with a solution that prevents denaturation or degradation of blood proteins found in a fecal sample, and sealing the sealable container. 3. A method of processing a fecal sample, the method comprising: a) obtaining a pair of portions of a fecal sample collected from a human subject, the pair of portions comprising: i) a sealed sealable vessel containing a first portion of a fecal sample and a stabilizing buffer; and ii) a sealed sealable container containing a second portion of a fecal sample and a solution that prevents denaturation or degradation of blood proteins found in a fecal sample, wherein the pair of portions are obtained by the method of claim 1; b) extracting nucleic acid from the first portion of the fecal sample; c) testing nucleic acid extracted from the first portion of the fecal sample for an amount of a human nucleic acid; and d) testing the second portion of the fecal sample for an amount of a blood protein present in the second portion of the fecal sample. Ex. 1001, 45:56–46:57, 46:64–47:15. Claims 4 and 12–19 depend, either directly or indirectly, from claim 3. Claims 4, 12, and 13 recite further limitations for testing nucleic acid, and claims 14–19 recite further limitations for testing for an amount of a blood protein. Id. at 47:17–18, 48:6–3
